problem_text: Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

problem.title: Многочлен
problem.statement: Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).
problem.solution:
Обозначим \(\Delta P(x)=P(x+1)-P(x)\). Условие переписывается как
\[
Q(x):=2P(x)-P(x+1)=P(x)-\Delta P(x),
\]
причём \(Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

**Решение 1.** Пусть \(\deg P=n\), тогда \(\Delta^{n+1}P\equiv0\). Применяя оператор \(\Delta^k\) к тождеству \(Q=P-\Delta P\), получаем
\[
\Delta^kQ=\Delta^kP-\Delta^{k+1}P
\]
при каждом \(k\ge0\). Складывая эти равенства для \(k=0,1,\ldots,n\), телескопическая сумма даёт
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n}\Delta^kQ=P-\Delta^{n+1}P=P.
\]
Поскольку \(Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\), целыми будут и все его конечные разности \(\Delta^kQ(m)\), а значит, \(P(m)\) — сумма целых чисел, то есть \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

**Решение 2.** Индукция по \(n=\deg P\).

При \(n=0\) многочлен \(P\equiv c\) и \(c=Q(0)\in\mathbb Z\).

Пусть \(n\ge1\) и утверждение верно для меньших степеней. Применяя \(\Delta\) к \(Q=P-\Delta P\), видим, что \(\Delta Q\) — результат той же операции \(R\mapsto R-\Delta R\), применённой к \(\Delta P\). При этом \(\Delta Q(m)\in\mathbb Z\) и \(\deg\Delta P=n-1\), поэтому по предположению индукции \(\Delta P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\), и тогда
\[
P(m)=Q(m)+\Delta P(m)\in\mathbb Z.
\]

**Решение 3.** Запишем \(P\) в биномиальном базисе
\[
\binom{x}{k}=\frac{x(x-1)\cdots(x-k+1)}{k!}:
\qquad
P(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{d}a_k\binom{x}{k},\qquad d=\deg P.
\]
Из
\[
\binom{x+1}{k}=\binom{x}{k}+\binom{x}{k-1}
\]
следует
\[
2\binom{x}{k}-\binom{x+1}{k}=\binom{x}{k}-\binom{x}{k-1},
\]
поэтому, при \(a_{d+1}:=0\),
\[
Q(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{d}(a_k-a_{k+1})\binom{x}{k}.
\]
Многочлен принимает целые значения во всех целых точках в точности тогда, когда все его коэффициенты в этом базисе целые. Применяя это к \(Q\), получаем \(a_k-a_{k+1}\in\mathbb Z\); по нисходящей индукции \(a_d,a_{d-1},\ldots,a_0\in\mathbb Z\), значит и \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для всех \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

system_prompt.text: Ты решаешь олимпиадные задачи строго и аккуратно. Сначала формализуй условие, введи обозначения и проверь все ограничения. Затем дай полное доказательство без скачков, перебора несуществующих случаев и неподтвержденных утверждений. Если используешь лемму, докажи ее. Не доказывай утверждение частным примером, если требуется доказательство для всех случаев. Перед финальным ответом проверь, что выбранные объекты действительно существуют при любых допустимых данных. В конце явно сформулируй финальный вывод. Не используй инструменты, поиск, код или калькуляторы.

Затем дай полное доказательство без скачков, перебора несуществующих случаев и неподтвержденных утверждений. Если используешь лемму, докажи ее. Не доказывай утверждение частным примером, если требуется доказательство для всех случаев. Перед финальным ответом проверь, что выбранные объекты действительно существуют при любых допустимых данных. В конце явно сформулируй финальный вывод. Не используй инструменты, поиск, код или калькуляторы.

Пусть \(P(x)\) — такой многочлен с вещественными коэффициентами, что \(2P(m)-P(m+1)\) является целым числом для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\). Докажите, что \(P(m)\in\mathbb Z\) для любого \(m\in\mathbb Z\).

results.answer:
### Setup and notation.

Let \( P(x) \in \mathbb{R}[x] \) be a polynomial such that \( 2P(m) - P(m+1) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \). Define the auxiliary polynomial \( S(x) := P(x+1) - 2P(x) \). Then \( S \in \mathbb{R}[x] \), and the given hypothesis immediately yields that \( S(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for all \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \). (Explicitly, \( S(m) = -(2P(m) - P(m+1)) \).) We shall work throughout with the forward difference operator \( \Delta \), defined by \( \Delta Q(x) = Q(x+1) - Q(x) \). Observe that \( S(x) = \Delta P(x) - P(x) = (\Delta - 1)P(x) \).

### Expansion in the binomial basis.

It is a standard fact that any polynomial taking integer values at all integers may be uniquely expressed in the binomial basis: specifically, since \( S \) maps \( \mathbb{Z} \) into \( \mathbb{Z} \), there exist integers \( c_k \) (vanishing for all sufficiently large \( k \)) such that \( S(x) = \sum_{k=0}^d c_k \binom{x}{k} \), where \( d = \deg S \). (Here \( \binom{x}{k} = \frac{x(x-1)\cdots(x-k+1)}{k!} \) for \( k \geq 1 \), with the usual convention \( \binom{x}{0} = 1 \).) Likewise, since \( P \) is itself a polynomial, it admits a unique expansion \( P(x) = \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \), where \( n = \deg P \) and the coefficients \( d_m \) are a priori real numbers.

### Relating the coefficients via the operator.

We now compute \( (\Delta - 1)P(x) \) in the binomial basis. Recall the standard identity \( \Delta \binom{x}{m} = \binom{x}{m-1} \) for \( m \geq 1 \) (with \( \Delta \binom{x}{0} = 0 \)). Accordingly, \( \Delta P(x) = \sum_{m=1}^n d_m \binom{x}{m-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Subtracting the expansion of \( P \) itself, we obtain \( (\Delta - 1)P(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} - \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \). Collecting like terms, the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{j} \) in this expression is \( d_{j+1} - d_j \) for each \( j = 0, \dots, n-1 \), and \( -d_n \) for the term \( j = n \) (with the convention that \( d_m = 0 \) for all \( m > n \)).

On the other hand, since \( (\Delta - 1)P = S \), equating coefficients with the binomial expansion of \( S \) yields the relations \( d_{j+1} - d_j = c_j \) for each \( j \geq 0 \), where \( c_j = 0 \) for all \( j > d \). In other words, we have the recurrence \( d_{j+1} = d_j + c_j \).

### Determination of the coefficients.

Iterating the recurrence, we find \( d_\ell = d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^{\ell-1} c_j \) for each \( \ell \geq 1 \). Since \( c_j = 0 \) for all \( j > d \), the sequence \( (d_j)_{j \geq 0} \) is eventually constant for \( j \geq d+1 \). But since \( P \) is a polynomial of finite degree, necessarily \( d_m = 0 \) for all sufficiently large \( m \), whence the eventual constant value of the sequence must vanish. The limiting value is \( d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^d c_j \), so we conclude \( d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^d c_j = 0 \), or equivalently \( d_0 = -\sum_{j=0}^d c_j \). As each \( c_j \) is an integer by construction, it follows that \( d_0 \in \mathbb{Z} \).

For arbitrary fixed \( \ell \), the expression \( d_\ell = d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^{\ell-1} c_j \) is then visibly a sum of integers, hence \( d_\ell \in \mathbb{Z} \). In this way, all coefficients \( d_m \) in the binomial expansion of \( P \) are integers.

### Conclusion.

It follows that \( P(x) = \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \) with each \( d_m \in \mathbb{Z} \). But each binomial coefficient \( \binom{m}{k} \) is itself an integer for \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \) and \( k \geq 0 \), whence \( P(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \), as desired.

(Note that this is consistent with the degrees: if \( \deg S = d \) with leading coefficient nonzero, then the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{d} \) in \( S \) is \( c_d \neq 0 \), and the corresponding relation \( d_{d+1} - d_d = c_d \) together with \( d_{d+1} = 0 \) forces \( d_d = -c_d \neq 0 \), so that \( \deg P = d \) as expected from the leading term analysis of \( S \).)

This completes the argument.

### Setup and notation.

Let \( P(x) \in \mathbb{R}[x] \) be a polynomial such that \( 2P(m) - P(m+1) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \). Define the auxiliary polynomial \( S(x) := P(x+1) - 2P(x) \). Then \( S \in \mathbb{R}[x] \), and the given hypothesis immediately yields that \( S(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for all \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \). (Explicitly, \( S(m) = -(2P(m) - P(m+1)) \).) We shall work throughout with the forward difference operator \( \Delta \), defined by \( \Delta Q(x) = Q(x+1) - Q(x) \). Observe that \( S(x) = \Delta P(x) - P(x) = (\Delta - 1)P(x) \).

### Expansion in the binomial basis.

It is a standard fact that any polynomial taking integer values at all integers may be uniquely expressed in the binomial basis: specifically, since \( S \) maps \( \mathbb{Z} \) into \( \mathbb{Z} \), there exist integers \( c_k \) (vanishing for all sufficiently large \( k \)) such that \( S(x) = \sum_{k=0}^d c_k \binom{x}{k} \), where \( d = \deg S \). (Here \( \binom{x}{k} = \frac{x(x-1)\cdots(x-k+1)}{k!} \) for \( k \geq 1 \), with the usual convention \( \binom{x}{0} = 1 \).) Likewise, since \( P \) is itself a polynomial, it admits a unique expansion \( P(x) = \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \), where \( n = \deg P \) and the coefficients \( d_m \) are a priori real numbers.

### Relating the coefficients via the operator.

We now compute \( (\Delta - 1)P(x) \) in the binomial basis. Recall the standard identity \( \Delta \binom{x}{m} = \binom{x}{m-1} \) for \( m \geq 1 \) (with \( \Delta \binom{x}{0} = 0 \)). Accordingly, \( \Delta P(x) = \sum_{m=1}^n d_m \binom{x}{m-1} = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} \). Subtracting the expansion of \( P \) itself, we obtain \( (\Delta - 1)P(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} d_{j+1} \binom{x}{j} - \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \). Collecting like terms, the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{j} \) in this expression is \( d_{j+1} - d_j \) for each \( j = 0, \dots, n-1 \), and \( -d_n \) for the term \( j = n \) (with the convention that \( d_m = 0 \) for all \( m > n \)).

On the other hand, since \( (\Delta - 1)P = S \), equating coefficients with the binomial expansion of \( S \) yields the relations \( d_{j+1} - d_j = c_j \) for each \( j \geq 0 \), where \( c_j = 0 \) for all \( j > d \). In other words, we have the recurrence \( d_{j+1} = d_j + c_j \).

### Determination of the coefficients.

Iterating the recurrence, we find \( d_\ell = d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^{\ell-1} c_j \) for each \( \ell \geq 1 \). Since \( c_j = 0 \) for all \( j > d \), the sequence \( (d_j)_{j \geq 0} \) is eventually constant for \( j \geq d+1 \). But since \( P \) is a polynomial of finite degree, necessarily \( d_m = 0 \) for all sufficiently large \( m \), whence the eventual constant value of the sequence must vanish. The limiting value is \( d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^d c_j \), so we conclude \( d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^d c_j = 0 \), or equivalently \( d_0 = -\sum_{j=0}^d c_j \). As each \( c_j \) is an integer by construction, it follows that \( d_0 \in \mathbb{Z} \).

For arbitrary fixed \( \ell \), the expression \( d_\ell = d_0 + \sum_{j=0}^{\ell-1} c_j \) is then visibly a sum of integers, hence \( d_\ell \in \mathbb{Z} \). In this way, all coefficients \( d_m \) in the binomial expansion of \( P \) are integers.

### Conclusion.

It follows that \( P(x) = \sum_{m=0}^n d_m \binom{x}{m} \) with each \( d_m \in \mathbb{Z} \). But each binomial coefficient \( \binom{m}{k} \) is itself an integer for \( m \in \mathbb{Z} \) and \( k \geq 0 \), whence \( P(m) \in \mathbb{Z} \) for every integer \( m \), as desired.

(Note that this is consistent with the degrees: if \( \deg S = d \) with leading coefficient nonzero, then the coefficient of \( \binom{x}{d} \) in \( S \) is \( c_d \neq 0 \), and the corresponding relation \( d_{d+1} - d_d = c_d \) together with \( d_{d+1} = 0 \) forces \( d_d = -c_d \neq 0 \), so that \( \deg P = d \) as expected from the leading term analysis of \( S \).)

This completes the argument.
